schools.summary: Gill: this fear is not a fear of wrath and damnation, nor a fear of diffidence and distrust of the power, grace, and goodness of God, but a cautious fear, a godly jealousy, a careful circumspection and watchfulness; Matthew Henry: our privileges by Christ under the gospel are not less than theirs under Moses, but greater, and so is our danger if we neglect; Paul charging that we fear, lest, a promise of entering into his rest being left us, any should seem to come short of it — the holy fear that guards against falling short of God's rest (Paul drawing the practical conclusion from Israel's fall: 'let us therefore fear,' not with a slavish dread but with a reverent, watchful caution, 'lest, a promise being left us of entering into his rest,' for the gospel rest is still held out, the promise yet standing open, 'any of you should seem to come short of it,' so that, since the rest remains to be entered and the promise is still ours, the great danger is not God's failing but our own falling short through unbelief, and against this a holy, vigilant fear is the proper safeguard).

themes: let us therefore fear
a promise being left us of entering into his rest
lest any of you should seem to come short of it
godly fear, not slavish dread, the safeguard

schools.summary: Gill: the gospel is the good news and glad tidings of salvation by Christ, and is said to be preached when men preach not themselves, nor read lectures of morality, nor mix law and gospel together, nor make justification and salvation to be by works; Paul declaring that the gospel was preached to us as well as to them, but the word did not profit them, not being mixed with faith in the hearers — the word unprofitable without faith mixing with it (Paul pressing the parallel between Israel and the present hearers: 'unto us was the gospel preached, as well as unto them,' a glad message of rest held out to the wilderness generation as it is to us, 'but the word preached did not profit them,' it bringing them no benefit and doing them no saving good, the reason being 'not being mixed with faith in them that heard it,' so the bare hearing of the word avails nothing unless it be received and blended with faith, faith being the necessary thing that makes the word profitable to the soul, its absence the cause of all Israel's loss).

themes: for unto us was the gospel preached, as well as unto them
the word preached did not profit them
not being mixed with faith in them that heard it
the bare hearing availing nothing without faith

schools.summary: Gill: this rest is not eternal rest only (which all believers shall enjoy, and they only) but spiritual rest in Christ under the gospel dispensation, into which believers now enter; Paul declaring that we which have believed do enter into rest, as God said the unbelievers should not, though the works were finished from the foundation of the world — the rest entered by faith, denied to unbelief (Paul confirming the necessity of faith from the contrast: 'we which have believed do enter into rest,' faith being the very door into the rest, so believers now enter the spiritual rest in Christ, whereas, as the oath showed, the unbelieving were sworn out, 'As I have sworn in my wrath, if they shall enter into my rest,' a Hebrew form meaning 'they shall not enter,' and adding a note that prepares his next argument, 'although the works were finished from the foundation of the world,' God's own rest having existed ever since creation, yet that primal rest not being the whole of what was meant).

themes: for we which have believed do enter into rest
As I have sworn in my wrath, if they shall enter into my rest
although the works were finished from the foundation of the world
faith the door into the rest, denied to unbelief

schools.summary: Barnes: at the close of the work of creation God rested — the work was done, that was the rest of God, who was happy in the contemplation of his own works and instituted the sabbath; Gill notes Moses, the penman, spoke (or God by him); Paul citing that God rested the seventh day from all his works — the original rest of God after creation as the first sense of rest (Paul, building his argument that a rest 'remains,' appealing first to the original rest: 'he spake in a certain place of the seventh day,' in Genesis, 'And God did rest the seventh day from all his works,' the rest of God himself who, having finished the work of creation, ceased and rested on the seventh day and hallowed it, so there is a 'rest of God' spoken of from the very beginning, the pattern and root of all the rest that follows, into which God invites his people to enter).

themes: for he spake in a certain place of the seventh day
And God did rest the seventh day from all his works
the original rest of God after creation
the pattern and root of all the rest

schools.summary: Gill: in this place (Psalm 95) God speaks again of another rest, distinct from that on the seventh day, and it is this latter, not the former, which believers under the gospel dispensation enter into; Paul citing again, 'If they shall enter into my rest' — the second rest spoken of in the Psalm, proving a further rest remains (Paul setting the two Scriptures side by side: the seventh-day rest of Genesis and now, 'in this place again,' in the Psalm written long after, the rest still spoken of as future and unentered, 'If they shall enter into my rest,' so that, since God in David's day still speaks of 'my rest' as a thing not yet entered, the original creation-rest cannot be the whole of what is meant, there being a further, distinct rest yet held out, which the argument will press home, the rest into which gospel believers enter).

themes: and in this place again
If they shall enter into my rest
the second, distinct rest still held out
a further rest remaining beyond the creation-rest

schools.summary: Barnes: since there is a rest spoken of in the Scriptures, implying that it is to be enjoyed by some, and since they to whom it was first promised did not inherit it, it follows that others are to partake of it; Gill: it follows by just consequence; Paul reasoning that, since it remains that some must enter the rest, and the first hearers entered not because of unbelief, the rest is still open — the rest left open for others since the first hearers forfeited it (Paul drawing the inference from the two Scriptures: 'it remaineth that some must enter therein,' since God's promise of rest cannot fail, some must certainly enter and enjoy it, but 'they to whom it was first preached,' the wilderness generation, 'entered not in because of unbelief,' forfeited it by their unbelief, so that, the first invited having fallen short, the rest stands open still, waiting to be entered by others, the promise not exhausted but reaching forward to a people who will believe).

themes: seeing therefore it remaineth that some must enter therein
they to whom it was first preached entered not in because of unbelief
the rest left open for others
the promise not exhausted but reaching forward

schools.summary: Gill: since the seventh day of the creation was a day of rest which God entered into and not man, and since the land of Canaan was a typical rest which the unbelieving Israelites did not enter because of unbelief, and yet there must be persons and a time for them to enter, God again limits a certain day; Paul observing that God again limits a certain day, 'Today,' in David, long after, saying, 'Today if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts' — the new day of opportunity fixed long after Canaan was given (Paul pressing the proof one step further: God 'limiteth,' appoints and marks out, 'a certain day, saying in David, To day,' and that 'after so long a time,' long after the wilderness and long after Canaan was possessed, in the days of David, God still naming a 'Today' of opportunity to hear his voice, so the existence of this fresh 'Today' in David's Psalm proves that the true rest was not the seventh-day rest, nor exhausted by the entry into Canaan, but still stood open, a present day of grace yet calling men to enter).

themes: again, he limiteth a certain day, saying in David, To day
after so long a time
the new day of opportunity fixed long after Canaan
the true rest not exhausted by Canaan, standing open

schools.summary: Gill: by 'Jesus' is meant Joshua, for Hosheah, Joshua, and Jesus are one and the same name (Joshua is called Jesus by the Septuagint, and by Josephus and Philo the Jew); Paul reasoning that if Joshua had given them rest, God would not afterward have spoken of another day — the proof that Canaan-rest under Joshua was not the true and final rest (Paul completing the proof: 'if Jesus,' that is Joshua who led Israel into Canaan, 'had given them rest,' had brought them into the full and final rest of God by settling them in the land, 'then would he not afterward have spoken of another day,' but God did, long afterward in David, speak of yet 'another day' of rest, and this proves that the rest Joshua gave, the earthly Canaan, was not the true and final rest but only a type and shadow of a better, spiritual, and eternal rest still to come, the rest that remains for the people of God).

debates.question: Was the rest Joshua gave in Canaan the true and final rest?

sides.position: No — since God afterward (in David) spoke of 'another day,' the earthly Canaan-rest under Joshua was only a type and shadow of a better, spiritual, and eternal rest still to come

themes: for if Jesus had given them rest
then would he not afterward have spoken of another day
Joshua's Canaan-rest not the final rest
the earthly Canaan a type of a better rest

schools.summary: Barnes: this is the conclusion to which the apostle comes, the result of the whole reasoning, that there is a rest distinct from the rest of creation and from the rest of Canaan, reserved for the people of God; Paul concluding that there remains a rest to the people of God — the great conclusion that a sabbath-rest remains for believers (Paul gathering the whole argument into its triumphant conclusion: 'there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God,' since the seventh-day rest was God's own, since Canaan under Joshua was not the final rest, and since God still spoke of a 'Today' in David, it follows that a true rest yet 'remaineth,' reserved and held in store, 'to the people of God,' to believers the true Israel; the word here properly a 'sabbath-rest,' a sabbatism, a settled, sabbath-like rest of the soul in God, begun in grace and consummated in glory, the abiding inheritance of all who believe).

themes: there remaineth therefore a rest
to the people of God
a sabbath-rest remaining for believers
begun in grace, consummated in glory

schools.summary: Gill: this is to be understood not of believers entering the gospel rest or eternal rest, but of the Lord Jesus Christ, for a single person is spoken of, not many, and the rest entered into is his own, which cannot be said of any other (and he that has entered has ceased from his own works as God did from his); Paul declaring that he that is entered into his rest has ceased from his own works, as God did from his — the rest as a ceasing from one's works after the pattern of God (Paul describing the character of this rest by analogy with God's: 'he that is entered into his rest,' Gill taking this chiefly of Christ who, having finished his work of redemption, entered his own rest as God ceased from creation, yet the principle applying to all who enter: to enter the rest is to have 'ceased from his own works, as God did from his,' to rest from the toil and labour of self-justifying works, leaning wholly on the finished work of another, even as God rested from creation having finished it, so gospel rest is a sabbath of the soul, ceasing from one's own works to rest in God's).

debates.question: Who is 'he that is entered into his rest'?

sides.position: Chiefly Christ, who having finished the work of redemption entered his own rest (a single person, the rest called 'his own'), as God ceased from creation
The believer, who in entering gospel rest ceases from his own self-justifying works to lean on the finished work of another

themes: for he that is entered into his rest
he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his
the rest a ceasing from one's own works
leaning on the finished work of another

schools.summary: Chrysostom: faith is indeed great and brings salvation, and without it it is not possible ever to be saved, yet it suffices not of itself to accomplish this, but there is need of a right conversation also, so Paul exhorts those who had already been counted worthy of the mysteries, 'let us labour to enter into that rest'; Paul charging that we labour to enter that rest, lest any fall after the same example of unbelief — the diligent striving to enter the rest, warned by Israel's example (Paul drawing the practical exhortation from the whole argument: since a rest remains, 'let us labour therefore to enter into that rest,' give all diligence, strive earnestly, a paradox to labour to enter a rest, pressing on by faith and obedience to lay hold of it, 'lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief,' lest any follow the wilderness generation in their fatal unbelief and so, like them, fall short and perish, so the way into the rest is by diligent, persevering faith, the warning of Israel's fall ever before us).

themes: let us labour therefore to enter into that rest
lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief
the diligent striving to enter the rest
faith and a right conversation both needed

schools.summary: Gill: this is to be understood of Christ, the essential Word of God, for 'the Word of God' was a known name of the Messiah among the Jews, and therefore the apostle makes use of it when writing to them, the words introduced as a reason why care should be taken; Paul declaring that the word of God is quick, powerful, sharper than a twoedged sword, piercing to dividing soul and spirit, and discerning the thoughts of the heart — the searching, dividing power of the word that lays the heart bare (Paul enforcing the warning by the searching power before which all stands open: 'the word of God is quick,' living, 'and powerful,' active and mighty, 'sharper than any twoedged sword,' piercing deeper than any blade, 'to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit, and of the joints and marrow,' reaching the innermost being, and 'is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart,' laying bare the secret motions of the soul; the debate whether 'the word of God' here is the personal Word, Christ himself, or the spoken and written word of God, both agreeing it searches and judges the heart).

debates.question: Is 'the word of God' here the personal Word (Christ) or the spoken word?

sides.position: The personal Word, Christ himself — 'the Word of God' a known name of the Messiah, who as the omniscient Lord discerns the thoughts of the heart (v.13 continuing of him)
The spoken and written word of God, living and powerful, which pierces and searches the heart

themes: for the word of God is quick, and powerful
sharper than any twoedged sword
a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart
the searching word laying the heart bare

schools.summary: Gill: Christ is the Lord God omniscient; there is no creature in general, rational or irrational, animate or inanimate, but what is known to him and seen by him, for all creatures are made and upheld by him and he is omnipresent; in particular the secrets of the heart are open to him; Paul declaring that no creature is hidden from his sight, but all things are naked and opened to the eyes of him with whom we have to do — the omniscient gaze before which nothing is hidden (Paul pressing the warning home with the all-seeing knowledge of God, or of Christ the Word: 'neither is there any creature that is not manifest in his sight,' nothing in all creation hidden from him, 'but all things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do,' every thing lying bare, exposed, laid open as a sacrifice flayed and split before the eyes of the One to whom we must give account, so that, since the Judge sees every secret of the heart, the warning against a hidden unbelief is the more solemn, there being no hiding from him with whom we have to do).

themes: neither is there any creature that is not manifest in his sight
all things are naked and opened
unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do
the omniscient gaze before which nothing is hidden

schools.summary: Gill: Christ is called a 'great' high priest because of the dignity of his person (as the Son of God), the virtue of his sacrifice, and the place where he now officiates as a priest, heaven itself, in distinction from the legal high priests who officiated in an earthly tabernacle; Paul declaring that, since we have a great high priest passed into the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, we should hold fast our profession — the great High Priest in heaven as the ground of steadfast profession (Paul turning from warning to encouragement, drawing comfort from Christ's priesthood: 'we have a great high priest,' not a mere earthly priest like Aaron but a 'great' one surpassing all, 'that is passed into the heavens,' gone up through the heavens into the very presence of God as the high priest into the holy place, and he is 'Jesus the Son of God,' truly man and truly God, and on this firm ground building the exhortation, 'let us hold fast our profession,' since we have so great and exalted a High Priest pleading for us in heaven, let us cling firmly to the faith we profess and not let it go).

themes: seeing then that we have a great high priest, that is passed into the heavens
Jesus the Son of God
let us hold fast our profession
the great High Priest the ground of steadfastness

schools.summary: Barnes: our High Priest is not cold and unfeeling; that is, we have one who is abundantly qualified to sympathize with us in our afflictions, having himself experienced our trials; Gill notes the saints have a high priest, but not one cruel and unmerciful; Paul declaring that we have not a high priest who cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities, but was in all points tempted as we are, yet without sin — the tender sympathy of a High Priest who shared our trials but not our sin (Paul commending the great High Priest by his tender fellow-feeling: 'we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities,' not a cold, distant priest unable to sympathize, but one who 'was in all points tempted like as we are,' who in his earthly life met the whole range of human trial, temptation, and suffering and so can feel for the tempted, 'yet without sin'; this crucial qualification, that Christ was tempted in every point truly and really yet never yielded and never sinned, his temptations leaving him spotless, so he is at once perfectly able to sympathize and perfectly holy).

themes: for we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities
was in all points tempted like as we are
yet without sin
tempted in all points, yet spotless and holy

schools.summary: Gill: coming to Christ, who is the high priest spoken of and was typified by the mercy seat (to which there seems an allusion), is very proper; saints come to him as a priest upon his throne for pardon and cleansing, for a justifying righteousness, for the supply of every need, and for grace and mercy; Paul inviting us to come boldly to the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need — the bold access to the throne of grace through the sympathizing High Priest (Paul drawing the sweetest practical conclusion from Christ's priesthood: 'let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace,' since we have such a High Priest, sympathizing and sinless, pleading above, we may draw near to God with confidence and freedom, not with slavish dread, to the very throne that, through Christ, is a 'throne of grace,' and that with a sure expectation, 'that we may obtain mercy,' the pardon of our sins, 'and find grace to help in time of need,' timely seasonable grace for every hour of trial, so the believer, through his great High Priest, has free and confident access to all the help of God).

themes: let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace
that we may obtain mercy
and find grace to help in time of need
bold access through the sympathizing High Priest
